speaker: interviewer
text: I'm conducting an interview with Rena Zabielak. We're in London, England. And it's Tape number four.

speaker: interviewee
text: What did I finish?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about making cigarettes for the Polish lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then she came. And she said, let us buy some piglets. It was in May. If you-- I-- if you will give me money, I will buy piglets. And later, we will have big pigs. And we will sell them. We will make money. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she going to keep these piglets?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had there a place. In the-- in that block of flats, she had plenty of space. Then we gave her money for piglets. Whatever she wanted she had from us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we decided that my mother with my uncle should come. Because it was a big, good hiding place on top. Then we all would be able to be together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day, Arnold went down. Because he always used to go out. Because he looked 100% not Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her boyfriend came up to him. And he said, you want to live? I also want to live. And I want you to pay me so and so much every month. And if you won't, I will announce you to the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But my Uncle Markusfeld, who was a bodyguard as I told you to the Marshal of Poland, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] one day he went out. And he met the higher officer what he was working under him as a bodyguard. And this man told him that he got places that we could hide. And as this was-- this one was blackmailing us, then he said he got outside Warsaw two places. And in one place is his wife with the children. And we would be able to stay there. And my Uncle Markusfeld with my mother would be in another place. We left this flat. And we went outside. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, Warsaw there, it was a big risk to go on the station. And-- and so when we arrived there-- and this landlord was a very nice man. But we had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were on an attic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on that attic, there was not floorboards. It was very low. It was just sand.

speaker: interviewer
text: They brought us two sacks with straw on which we could sleep. And he used to bring us food upstairs. You know what? I don't remember how we went to the toilet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day, this officer, his name was Yurek, he arrived. And in the evening, we used to go to Yurek's wife to straighten ourselves out. Because we were sitting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we went to-- to her, we had like a-- for an hour or so. And then we went back on that attic. One day that Yurek arrived, and he said he doesn't feel well. He got a cold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he gave us a drink. And he had a drink. But out-- in our drink, he put in some--

speaker: interviewer
text: A drug?

speaker: interviewee
text: --drug. He drugged us. And after this, we slept for 48 hours. And all the money what we had he took away. When we woke up, Arnold looked around. All the money was gone. But this ring what I am wearing, he bought this when he was building in the ghetto the wall in the [NON-ENGLISH]. He bought a-- a stone. And he sewed it into the fly of his trousers. And he never found this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But Arnold knew that we've got to get--

speaker: interviewer
text: Away?

speaker: interviewee
text: --away from there. But he wanted some money back. And he told the wife-- he was sitting there. He found a knife. And he was sharpening the knife. And he said, if you won't give me half of the money, I can't live without the money. I will kill him. See, I've got a knife. And funny enough, they gave us half of the money back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we ran away from there and went back there to that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: The original flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: The original flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother with you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we had to pay money to the blackmailers. Well, we were lucky. Because we told them that we have-- if they would know that we've got money on us, we wouldn't be alive. But Arnold said that there is a bank manager who gives us money every few weeks. And he used to go out like pretend that he's going for the money. And when he used to go out, he also had funny experiences. Because he went to that Pole what he was a friend of my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] father's.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when he went out and left you behind? Were you worried?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He only went out for a few hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were you worried?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very worried. But he didn't look Jewish at all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day, he went out. He came to that Pole. And that Pole said to him, let us go to have a drink. He didn't want to go to have a drink. But if the Pole says comes, then he has to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: They went into a pub. And there comes another Pole. And he says, you know what happened to me? I am now giving vodka to all of you. He said, why? I am giving vodka to all of you, because came to me a Jew. And he said he wants I should hide him. I said, why not? And he will give me money.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him to come. I collected the money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I ask, and I went out. I rang up the Germans. They came.

speaker: interviewer
text: They pull off his trousers. They saw he is Jewish. And here, in those ruins, they killed him. Let us have another drink. Can you imagine Arnold's heart when he was sitting there and he was telling this story, how he felt? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then another time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they start bombing Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not much, but you had to put on dark paper. And the Poles ask Arnold to fix it. He was fixing. He was on the ladder. He fell down. And his arm came out of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dislocated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dislocated. He was, again, by that Pole. And they ask-- and the German-- the Germans came. And they said to everybody, hands up. He couldn't take out his hand. With the other hand, had to pull it up. Because it was dislocated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our little girl, Arnold's-- this little girl which went out was by that Polish woman. And when we went into the country, we first went there where the little girl was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whose little girl was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arnold's little girl. And we saw that she looks ill [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then. And that Pole had two children. And he said, they are going to a little hotel in the country for children. And we said, it would be a good idea if she would go with the children. And they said it will cost so and so much money that she should go. We said, all right. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She was there in the country, and we were pleased. I went out one day with-- you see, I didn't have a coat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had a coat, but it had to be altered. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then [INAUDIBLE] daughter went with me to a dressmaker to alter me the coat. And when I came back, I was stopped by two women. They hit me. And everything what I wear-- had on me they took away. They blackmailed me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have on you that they took?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a gold bracelet, a watch, a chain. I didn't know that I would be black-- and all the money what I had they took away, and they hit me. I knew that she'd done it, but I couldn't help myself. The same time, the children came back from-- from the holiday. And the little girl-- her name was at the time [INAUDIBLE]-- didn't come back with them. Then we were very worried. And Arnold went there. And he said he must have her back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she said, no, she's OK and so on. He said, no, she must come back. And when they brought her back, she looked like a skeleton. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we couldn't take her to us, because we had to leave the place. Because I was blackmailed again. Then we had to leave and to go to that flat, to that old woman's flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: The original flat.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the flat where mommy was there with Markusfeld. When we were in that flat, we were under padlock and key. We couldn't walk. We couldn't cook. And when you went to the toilet, you were scared to pull the chain. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in the evening, once in two or three days, they used to come. Then we pulled the chain. It was very, very hard there. And we decided still to go back to the blackmailers. Because that-- you know, what they blackmailed me and so on, it was still better than here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who was the little girl's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: She got killed, what I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she was left with us. And the other girl was killed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Us, we went back. We brought the little girl to us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Markusfeld took her to a specialist, to a pediatrician. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the pediatrician's name was Professor Schenreich. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said that she got beginning of tuberculosis.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was she at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: About seven, eight. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she had a head full of lice. And the fingers were full of pus, because she didn't eat. And she wasn't in a hotel like they told her. They gave her to a beggar who was begging by the church. And they paid very little for her. And the beggar used to collect pieces of cabbage, potatoes on the market and this to cook her soup. And she was starving. And we paid to that old woman a lot of money for keeping her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were looking after her for a time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We put her right. And we knew that if the child will be with us, in case we all be caught, why should she lose her life? And we knew another caretaker. And she didn't look Jewish at all. The child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we gave her to the caretaker and paid for her. And she was by that caretaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she look after her well?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was looking after her quite well. She used to learn like to go-- she didn't go to school. Because I don't think there were schools. But she used to go to a teacher to learn. And she used even to learn to play piano. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yes. Then we were-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] and we were there. It was Christmas. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then when it was getting warm, I didn't feel well. I couldn't catch the breath. Then Arnold decided that I've got to get a place, that I should look after children or something to be in fresh. That Pole had two little children. And I went there, left them, my mother and Arnold. And I went to that Pole to look after the children. But at night, I used to go to that woman, where [INAUDIBLE] was. But she-- [INAUDIBLE] was already in the country. Then I slept there at night. And in the morning, I used to go back to my work. One day, I went there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on the way, in the evening, she followed me. I went on the tram. And I saw that she's-- and there were two trams. One was for the Poles and was for the Germans. This old woman went on the tram for the Germans. And she was showing them-- and I saw it on a distant-- that I was there. I run down from the tram. And I hid in a block of flats there. And when I saw that she left, I went to that Polish woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I was walking to the Polish woman, I met the blackmailer who blackmailed me before. And as I knew that she's a blackmailer, then I said to her, look, here. I haven't got much money now. But can you find me a place where I could hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she said, yes. And we made arrangements for Sunday morning that I will meet her somewhere. And I will be able [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she would get me a place. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But she lived in the same block of flats what my family was there. I'd-- I had to go back to them to tell them what happened. And we lived on the second floor. And it was very funny. We had a sign if somebody went out. And we had a little window. And the window was open. And on the window, if we put on a white handkerchief hanger, it means you can come in. And I went back. I told them the story. And they said, I've got to leave. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] As they told me that I've got to leave-- in-- in that flat, what that old woman was, they used to meet the Polish underground government. All the communists used to meet there. And they gave me also-- I had different papers. Well, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- one of the Communists said that he would-- he's-- he was in the underground, they were. And his mother and sister-in-law lived outside Warsaw. And they said that they would keep me. And I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. I went there. And I was in their flat. And one day, the Germans came. And I start laughing, because they didn't look for a Jew. And they had-- she had a sister. And the sister had two women and a child. And one woman was the [NON-ENGLISH], which came to the house to take blood from my cut. And I was there on a-- I had to leave, because I didn't want to risk them and to risk me. And I was there on the second floor in the flat. It was an attic. And we watched through the window. And they were bombing Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] saw the Germans running out of Warsaw. And they had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the rifles on a string, without shoes. It was a pleasure to watch them. They were running away.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in September, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All of a sudden, a bomb fell outside our flat, and we had to leave. Then I said to the ladies, let us go down to the shelter. But we shouldn't be together. Because if they will catch us, they will kill us, the Poles will. And when we went, a German was standing outside with a machine gun shooting to the Russians. And in the morning, about 12 o'clock, walked in a Polish officer. And we were free. That was the 18th of September, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were just outside Warsaw at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was in Grochow. And I didn't know what happened to my mother, to my uncles, and to the little girl. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in the house that time. I went to the house of that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] higher officer, what he was in the underground. And they came back. He was a colonel in the army. And he was the chief of police of District Warsaw. And his brother was working also in the police, because they were in the underground. And here they came. They were higher officers. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was there with them. And they gave me a job [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to work for UNRRA. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And together with me were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Dr. Savitski. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Savitski later was one of the proc-- proc-- how do they call those? In Nuremberg the-- he wasn't-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot how do you say it. If somebody is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot.
